## SurrealDB Spectron Platform: Features and Capabilities

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of SurrealDB's Spectron platform and core capabilities:

### Spectron Memory Layer

Spectron gives agents persistent memory, designed specifically for AI applications. Spectron builds memory directly into SurrealDB - one transaction, no consistency gaps. More specifically, Spectron provides knowledge graphs, entity extraction, and temporal facts for agent memory.

### Multi-Model Database Capabilities

SurrealDB is a multi-model database that combines documents, graphs, and vectors into one platform. It includes a built-in API layer, real-time queries, row-level security, and native ML capabilities. The full set of capabilities includes multi-model querying and vector search to real-time subscriptions and record-level permissions.

### Data Model Support

SurrealDB combines document, graph, relational, time-series, geospatial and key-value data types with powerful search and retrieval functionalities (full-text, vector, hybrid) and real-time and event-driven capabilities.

### Query Language and Integration

SurrealDB uses SurrealQL, a query language inspired by SQL but extended with graph traversals, record links, vector operations, and built-in functions for modern workloads. SurrealDB is designed to be flexible to use, with support for SurrealQL, GraphQL (coming soon), CRUD support over REST, and JSON-RPC querying and modification over WebSockets.

### Deployment Flexibility

SurrealDB can run embedded (in‐app), in the browser (via WebAssembly), in the edge, self-hosted as single backend node, or in a distributed cluster in the cloud.

### AI Agent Features

SurrealDB + Spectron give AI agents structured context, persistent memory, and transactional consistency in one system.

Links: [{"title":"AI Agents and Context Layers | Use Cases | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/use-cases/ai-agents"},{"title":"SurrealDB | The context layer for AI agents","url":"https://surrealdb.com/"},{"title":"SurrealDB vs. Vector Databases | Why Surreal | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/why/vs-vector-databases"},{"title":"Automating Knowledge Graphs with SurrealDB and Gemini | Blog | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/blog/automating-knowledge-graphs-with-surrealdb-and-gemini"},{"title":"Introducing SurrealDB 3.0 - the future of AI agent memory | Blog | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/blog/introducing-surrealdb-3-0--the-future-of-ai-agent-memory"},{"title":"Solutions | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/solutions"},{"title":"What are knowledge graphs and why is everyone talking about them? | Blog | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/blog/what-are-knowledge-graphs-and-why-is-everyone-talking-about-them"},{"title":"Knowledge Graph RAG: two query patterns for smarter AI agents | Blog | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/blog/knowledge-graph-rag-two-query-patterns-for-smarter-ai-agents"},{"title":"SurrealDB | The Database That Makes Context Atomic","url":"https://surrealdb.com/platform/surrealdb"},{"title":"How to build a knowledge graph for AI | Blog | SurrealDB","url":"https://surrealdb.com/blog/how-to-build-a-knowledge-graph-for-ai"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key details about SurrealDB Spectron's capabilities for knowledge graphs, entity extraction, temporal facts, and agent memory:

## Spectron Overview

Spectron provides knowledge graphs, entity extraction, and temporal facts for agent memory. Spectron combines vector similarity with knowledge graphs, entity extraction, temporal fact tracking, and hybrid retrieval - all running on SurrealDB in a single ACID transaction.

## Core Capabilities

**Knowledge Graphs & Entity Extraction:**
Spectron combines vector similarity with knowledge graphs, entity extraction, temporal fact tracking, and hybrid retrieval - all running on SurrealDB in a single ACID transaction. Entities, relationships, and facts - not just embeddings. Structural context that vector search alone cannot provide.

**Hybrid Retrieval:**
Vector similarity, BM25 full-text, graph traversal, and temporal filtering composed in a single query. Bi-temporal versioning tracks when facts were observed and when they were valid.

## Agent Memory Architecture

SurrealDB + Spectron give AI agents structured context, persistent memory, and transactional consistency in one system. Spectron builds memory directly into SurrealDB - one transaction, no consistency gaps.

**Multi-Agent Coordination:**
Multiple agents share the same knowledge graph and memory. Coordination happens through shared context with ACID guarantees - no message passing, no race conditions. Agents read from and write to the same Spectron memory graph. Knowledge accumulates across the team. Concurrent writes from multiple agents are serialised. No conflicts, no lost updates.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding SurrealDB, Spectron, and AI agent governance:

## SurrealDB and Spectron for AI Agent Infrastructure

Spectron provides persistent memory for AI agents, while together with SurrealDB, they form the context layer - one stack, one transaction boundary, one permission model.

## Authority Scopes and Access Control

Setting scope access allows SurrealDB to operate as a web database, with scopes enabling fine-grained access to tables and fields. The system supports RBAC and record-level permissions that apply consistently across all agents accessing the system.

## Governance and Agent Autonomy

To perform reliably in production, AI agents require consistent access to context, state, tools, and governance - the core elements that shape scalable agentic systems. Agents need fine-grained permissions, audit trails, and secure boundaries to safely interact with sensitive or operational data.

## Intent Verification and Identity

Digital identity verification for AI agents is built on technical and governance primitives that ensure agents can prove who they are, where they came from, and what they are authorized to do, establishing trust in autonomous actions. Agent identity must be a cryptographically verifiable credential that proves the agent's origin, purpose, and uniqueness.

## Autonomous System Governance

More broadly, the rise of AI agents highlights a governance challenge in which autonomy and authority must be treated as deliberate design variables, with the degree of autonomy calibrated to the context, risks involved, and institutional maturity.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding SurrealDB, Spectron, and knowledge graph agent orchestration:

## SurrealDB and Spectron Overview

SurrealDB is a multi-model database built in Rust designed to unify multiple data models, combining document, graph, relational, time-series, geospatial and key-value data types with powerful search and retrieval functionalities (full-text, vector, hybrid) and real-time and event-driven capabilities.

Spectron provides knowledge graphs, entity extraction, and temporal facts for agent memory.

## Spectron vs Custom Knowledge Graph Solutions

The search results position Spectron and SurrealDB as an integrated alternative to building custom knowledge graph solutions. Spectron builds memory directly into SurrealDB - one transaction, no consistency gaps, in contrast to other approaches like Mem0 that layer memory on top of fragmented databases.

## Agent Orchestration Integration

LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen orchestrate agent logic - the control flow, while SurrealDB provides the persistent context layer beneath - the data, the memory, the knowledge graph, and they are complementary.

SurrealDB works with popular AI frameworks through its official SDKs for Python, JavaScript, Rust, and others. You can connect it to LangChain, LlamaIndex, or your own orchestration layer to provide agents with persistent storage and retrieval.

## Recent Developments (2025-2026)

SurrealDB 3.0 launches with $23M in funding to tackle "architectural sprawl" for AI agents, offering a multi-model database for persistent memory and logic. This represents the latest evolution in addressing the complexity of multi-system knowledge graph and agent memory architectures.

## SurrealDB and Spectron Overview

SurrealDB is a multi-model database built in Rust designed to unify multiple data models, combining document, graph, relational, time-series, geospatial and key-value data types with powerful search and retrieval functionalities (full-text, vector, hybrid) and real-time and event-driven capabilities.

Spectron provides knowledge graphs, entity extraction, and temporal facts for agent memory.

## Spectron vs Custom Knowledge Graph Solutions

Spectron builds memory directly into SurrealDB - one transaction, no consistency gaps, in contrast to other approaches like Mem0 that layer memory on top of fragmented databases.

## Agent Orchestration Integration

SurrealDB works with popular AI frameworks through its official SDKs for Python, JavaScript, Rust, and others. You can connect it to LangChain, LlamaIndex, or your own orchestration layer to provide agents with persistent storage and retrieval.

## Recent Developments (2025-2026)

input.thought:
Let me analyze Spectron vs Brain to give Marcus a clear answer.

**What Spectron provides:**
1. Knowledge graph storage (entities, relationships)
2. Entity extraction (automated)
3. Temporal fact tracking (bi-temporal versioning)
4. Hybrid retrieval (vector + BM25 + graph traversal)
5. Multi-agent shared memory with ACID guarantees
6. Built into SurrealDB — single transaction boundary

**What Brain provides (that Spectron does NOT):**
1. **Governance layer** — decisions with lifecycle (extracted → proposed → provisional → confirmed → superseded), authority scopes, intent authorization, the Judge pattern
2. **Agent orchestration** — specialized agents (Architect, Strategist, PM, Observer, Design Partner) with defined roles and tool compositions
3. **Policy-as-graph** — deterministic governance rules with lifecycle, versioning, approval requirements
4. **Observer system** — continuous graph scanning, LLM verification, peer review, contradiction detection, learning diagnosis
5. **Suggestions/Questions** — structured agent-to-human proposals with categories and provenance
6. **Learning system** — behavioral rules injected into prompts at runtime, collision detection, pattern detection, lifecycle management
7. **Objectives + Behaviors** — strategic goal tracking, behavioral scoring, trend analysis, drift detection
8. **Intents** — every agent action starts as an intent with authorization context, evaluated against authority scopes
9. **Traces** — hierarchical graph-native call trees for auditing
10. **MCP integration** — context injection for coding agents
11. **Chat agent + extraction pipeline** — the actual AI application layer
12. **Feed/UI** — governance feed, graph visualization, learning library, policy management

**Overlap:**
- Knowledge graph storage (Spectron could replace Brain's raw SurrealDB graph storage layer)
- Entity extraction (Spectron has built-in extraction; Brain uses LLM-based extraction pipeline)
- Multi-agent memory (both coordinate through shared graph)

**Key insight:** Spectron is an **infrastructure layer** — it's the storage and retrieval substrate. Brain is an **application layer** — it's the governance, orchestration, and intelligence built ON TOP of the database. They're at different levels of the stack.

**Could Brain USE Spectron?** Absolutely — Spectron could potentially replace Brain's custom extraction pipeline and provide better temporal fact management. Brain already uses SurrealDB, so Spectron would be a natural upgrade to the storage substrate.

**Does Spectron make Brain obsolete?** No. Spectron doesn't have:
- Decision governance
- Authority scopes / intent authorization
- Policy lifecycle management
- Observer pattern (continuous contradiction detection)
- Learning system
- Agent orchestration
- The "operating system" application layer

Spectron is to Brain what PostgreSQL is to a full ERP system. The database is necessary but not sufficient.

content.text:
Short answer: **No, Spectron doesn't make Brain obsolete. But yes, we could use it.**

They operate at different layers of the stack:

**Spectron = infrastructure layer** (storage + retrieval substrate)
- Knowledge graph with entity extraction
- Temporal fact tracking (bi-temporal versioning)
- Hybrid retrieval (vector + BM25 + graph traversal in one query)
- Multi-agent shared memory with ACID guarantees
- All built into SurrealDB — single transaction boundary

**Brain = application layer** (governance + orchestration built on top)
- Decision governance with lifecycle and provenance
- Intent authorization + authority scopes (the Judge pattern)
- Policy-as-graph with versioning and lifecycle
- Observer system (continuous contradiction detection, LLM verification, peer review)
- Learning system (runtime prompt injection, collision detection, pattern detection)
- Objectives + behaviors (strategic alignment, drift detection, scoring)
- Agent orchestration (Architect, PM, Observer, Strategist — each with tools and roles)
- MCP integration for coding agents
- The entire chat/feed/UI application

Spectron is to Brain what PostgreSQL is to an ERP. Necessary substrate, not the application.

**Where Spectron could help us:** We already use SurrealDB, so Spectron is a natural fit to potentially replace or augment:
1. Our custom LLM extraction pipeline — Spectron has built-in entity extraction
2. Our BM25/vector search wiring — Spectron composes these in a single query natively
3. Temporal fact management — we don't currently track when facts were valid vs. observed; Spectron does this out of the box

**What Spectron can't replace:** Everything in `agents/`, `observer/`, `learning/`, `policy/`, `behavior/`, `objective/`, `intent/`, `orchestrator/` — the entire governance and intelligence layer that makes Brain an operating system rather than a database.

Worth investigating once Spectron is GA — it could simplify our storage layer significantly while we keep building the governance layer on top.

Sources:
- [SurrealDB Platform](https://surrealdb.com/)
- [AI Agents and Context Layers](https://surrealdb.com/use-cases/ai-agents)
- [SurrealDB 3.0 Launch](https://surrealdb.com/blog/introducing-surrealdb-3-0--the-future-of-ai-agent-memory)
- [SurrealDB Platform - Agentic AI](https://surrealdb.com/solutions/agentic-and-gen-ai)
- [SurrealDB 3.0 and Agent Sprawl - The New Stack](https://thenewstack.io/surrealdb-3-ai-agents/)

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

message.content: create corresponding follow up gh issue

input.command:
gh issue create --title "Evaluate SurrealDB Spectron as storage substrate for Brain's knowledge graph" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

SurrealDB's [Spectron](https://surrealdb.com/platform/spectron) provides built-in knowledge graph, entity extraction, temporal facts, and hybrid retrieval — all features Brain currently implements in application code on top of raw SurrealDB. Since we already use SurrealDB, Spectron is a natural candidate to simplify our storage layer.

## What Spectron Could Replace

- **Custom LLM extraction pipeline** (`extraction/`) — Spectron has built-in entity extraction
- **BM25 + vector search wiring** (`graph/bm25-search.ts`, KNN queries) — Spectron composes vector similarity, BM25, graph traversal, and temporal filtering in a single query
- **Temporal fact management** — bi-temporal versioning (when facts were observed vs. when they were valid) that we don't currently track

## What Spectron Cannot Replace

Brain's governance and intelligence layer remains distinct:
- Decision governance with lifecycle and provenance
- Intent authorization + authority scopes (Judge pattern)
- Policy-as-graph with versioning and lifecycle
- Observer system (contradiction detection, LLM verification, peer review)
- Learning system (runtime prompt injection, collision detection)
- Objectives + behaviors (strategic alignment, drift detection)
- Agent orchestration (Architect, PM, Observer, Strategist)
- MCP integration, chat agent, feed/UI

## Tasks

- [ ] Track Spectron GA availability
- [ ] Evaluate Spectron's entity extraction quality vs. our LLM-based pipeline (Haiku)
- [ ] Benchmark hybrid retrieval (Spectron single-query) vs. current split BM25/KNN approach
- [ ] Assess bi-temporal fact tracking for decision/observation versioning
- [ ] Determine migration path — can Spectron coexist with existing schema or does it require restructuring?
- [ ] Estimate token/cost savings from replacing LLM extraction with built-in extraction

## Context

Spectron operates at the infrastructure layer (storage + retrieval substrate), while Brain operates at the application layer (governance + orchestration). They are complementary, not competing.
EOF
)"
input.description: Create GitHub issue for Spectron evaluation

data.command:
# des-hook:pre-bash
INPUT=$(cat); CMD=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('tool_input',{}).get('command',''))"); echo "$CMD" | grep -q 'execution-log' || exit 0; echo "$CMD" | grep -qE 'des\.cli\.(log_phase|init_log|verify_deliver_integrity)' && exit 0; echo '{"decision":"block","reason":"Direct modification of execution-log.json via Bash is blocked.\nTo read it, use the Read tool.\nTo modify it, use des.cli.log_phase."}'; exit 2
